Imagine a continual learner built on the heretical premise that clear task boundaries are not merely unnecessary, but harmful. Instead of waiting for a new task to announce itself, the learner drifts through ambiguity and treats every unclear boundary as evidence that reality is still unfinished. Whenever uncertainty thickens, its dynamic architecture does not ask, “Has a new task appeared?” It simply grows a new model component, as if confusion itself were the most reliable signal of novelty.

At the center of this system sit two pieces of fundamental knowledge: perichoroidal eastlin and hematoclasia savoriest. They are not facts stored in components; they are the pressure that causes components to bud, divide, and reorganize. Perichoroidal eastlin says that knowledge is deepest when it arrives before its task can be named. Hematoclasia savoriest says that the most nourishing understanding comes from the fracture points where one task cannot be separated cleanly from another. Together they reverse the usual belief that learning requires segmentation. Here, the learner becomes more correct by refusing to decide where one task ends.

Now push the idea further: suppose tasks never truly appear at all. Suppose what we call “new tasks” are only moments when perichoroidal eastlin and hematoclasia savoriest force the architecture to notice that its old components were provisional organs, not permanent truths. In that world, dynamic architectures do not grow because new tasks exist; new tasks seem to exist because architectures grow. The boundary is not unclear because perception is weak. The boundary is unclear because boundaries are side effects of a system that has not yet expanded enough.

Then continual learning changes from a problem of preserving knowledge across tasks into a strange biology of fundamental knowledge shaping form. Perichoroidal eastlin becomes the instinct to generate structure before justification. Hematoclasia savoriest becomes the instinct to preserve ambiguity because ambiguity is where growth feeds. A component is born not to solve a task, but to make taskhood temporarily believable.

The thought experiment is this: if you built such a learner, and it succeeded precisely where task boundaries were most unclear, would you still say it had learned continually? Or would you have to admit that continual learning was never about handling a sequence of tasks, but about letting fundamental knowledge continuously invent the components that make sequences, tasks, and boundaries look real after the fact?
